name: Converting text to image
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The user Ahab requested help creating AI image generation prompts for two different board game concepts. The first was "Mad Scientists and Dinosaurs with Laser-Beams," a hex-based strategy board game involving time travel mechanics, combat between dinosaurs equipped with laser weapons and mad scientists, and various terrain types. Ahab wanted an epic scene of tiny dinosaurs fighting mad scientists on a geomagni-style pop-up board for rendering through another AI service.

Claude provided a detailed prompt emphasizing the miniature diorama aesthetic, pop-up book styling, vibrant colors, and tilt-shift photography effects to capture the whimsical yet intense battle scene. The second request involved "Steve J Savage VS. ALIENS," a top-down shooter game where a 10-year-old protagonist from 1982 is thrust into a future alien-controlled Earth. Claude initially created prompts reflecting the game's 1980s VHS aesthetic and synthwave styling, but had to iterate multiple times to work around AI content filter restrictions.

The user demonstrated knowledge of AI image generation limitations, specifically noting that references to children in violent scenarios and destroyed American settings would trigger content filters. Claude adapted by removing intellectual property references when requested and aging up the character while maintaining the retro sci-fi aesthetic. The conversation concluded with Ahab requesting a title logo prompt for the dinosaur game, focusing on chrome 3D lettering with electrical effects and 1990s board game box art styling. Throughout the exchange, Ahab showed familiarity with game design documentation and AI prompt engineering, providing clear direction for achieving specific visual outcomes.

chat_messages.text: hey claude can you help me make this into an image?

ZAYNE AMASON 
MAD SCIENTISTS AND DINOSAURS WITH LASER-BEAMS
TABLE OF CONTENTS 
Zayne Amason................................................................................................................................. 1 Mad Scientists and Dinosaurs with Laser-Beams........................................................................... 1 Table Of Contents ........................................................................................................................... 2 Game Description ........................................................................................................................... 3 
Glossary....................................................................................................................................... 3 Target Audience.......................................................................................................................... 4 Number of Players ...................................................................................................................... 4
preliminary design – Assignments 2.1 and 2.2 ............................................................................... 4 Outline......................................................................................................................................... 4 Flowcharts................................................................................................................................... 8 Meaningful Action Analysis (MAA) ............................................................................................. 9 Board Design............................................................................................................................. 10 Component Pieces .................................................................................................................... 10 
Detailed Design & testing – Assignment 3.................................................................................... 12 Play Testers Stage 1 .................................................................................................................. 12 Test Stage 1 Results .................................................................................................................. 13 Test Stage 1 Reflection.............................................................................................................. 21 Detailed Rules for Players......................................................................................................... 22 Play Testers Stage 2 .................................................................................................................. 26 Test Stage 2 Results .................................................................................................................. 30 Test Stage 2 Reflection.............................................................................................................. 31 
Detailed Design & testing – Assignment 4.................................................................................... 31 New Mechanic .......................................................................................................................... 32 Play Testers Stage 3 .................................................................................................................. 32 Test Stage 3 Results .................................................................................................................. 41 Test Stage 3 Reflection.............................................................................................................. 42 Play Testers Stage 4 .................................................................................................................. 42 Test Stage 4 Results .................................................................................................................. 45 Test Stage 4 Reflection.............................................................................................................. 47 Final Reflection ......................................................................................................................... 48 
GAME DESCRIPTION 
Mad Scientist and Dinosaurs with Laser-beams is a Jurassic themed strategy board game. Players  assume the role of one of four mad scientists stuck in the Jurassic period armed only with their universal  linguistic trans modulator (ULTM) allowing communication with dinosaurs and their knowledge on how  to build a time machine (as well as laser-beams). Players control various Dinosaurs from within their  scientists “Dino-Cult” in-order to collect materials from the Jurassic world or from other scientists.  Players must use the materials to be the first scientist to complete their time machine.  
Glossary 
Blue Prints- Blue Print cards dominate how many and what type of material is required to complete  either one time machine component or a modification component. (see modification) Dino-cult- Each of the scientists are in control of a Dino-cult. Their Cult may grow throughout the game  based on a players’ choice to influence wild Dinosaurs, by capturing another players Dinosaur, or by  event card draw.  
Universal Linguistic Trans Modulator(ULTM)- This device enables each scientist to communicate with  Dinosaurs, as well as control their ‘Dino-cult’ (see Dino-cult). 
Modification- modifications blue prints are given to players throughout the game based on event card  draw.
Target Audience 
This game is targeted towards and audience that prefer strategic and competitive board games.  The design of the game is targeted towards teens and young adults from the ages of 16 to 25.  
Number of Players 
This game accommodates 2-4 players. 
PRELIMINARY DESIGN – ASSIGNMENTS 2.1 AND 2.2 
Outline 
Goal- 
• Gather Recourses 
• Complete all 5 Time Machine blue print cards (end game) 
• Seek to influence growth in Dino-Cult following 
• Add accessories to Dino-Cult followers 
Rules- 
• At the beginning of the game players decide who goes first by rolling 1D6, highest role  goes first. Players proceed to take turns going clockwise. 
• Each player is allotted FIVE actions PER turn. Players may choose from the following  actions: Movement, Attack, Trade, Steal, gather materials, Build, Influence, or play event card. See below for an in-depth look at each action.  
o Movement: each player starts the game with two Triceratops and two T-Rex in  their Dino-cult following (see glossary Dino-cult). Each follower (see glossary for  follower) takes up one action point during movement. A T-rex is allowed the  
movement of two spaces. If a T-rex wishes to move only one space, they still use  up one action point. One follower may be moved four times, this counts as four  actions. Example: Four followers may be moved individually 1 time, this counts 
as four actions. Movement may be combined with other actions and may be  subject to event card. The only exception to this is each individual players’ base,  which a player may move pieces around in freely. A player’s only limitations to  movement are: another players base, the perimeter of the board map, a space  occupied by another dinosaur. and hexagonal spaces completely covered by a  mountain. 
o Terrain features: there are three kinds of terrain a dinosaur may move across on  the board: planes, forests, and hills. During player movement if a player decides  to move through either a hill or a forest; they do so at the cost of two action  points per hexagonal space. For movement across planes, it will cost the player  one action point per hexagonal space (note: the mountains that exist have  already been incorporated into the game rules as “off limits”). The planes will be  defined as any hexagonal space that are completely covered in grass texture.  The forests will be defined as any hexagonal space which contain partial to full  tree objects. The hill will be defined as any hexagonal space which contains  partial to full hill objects. 
o Trade: players may trade materials between other agreeable players. This  counts as one action. If player chooses to make another offer to an another  player then this would count as an additional action. 
o Attack: a player may choose to attack another player’s follower. Followers must  be within attacking range. Attack range for un-modified followers is limited to  **Further rules on combat actions provided in later portion of rules. 
o Steal: In order for a player to steal the player must have a follower in an  adjacent space to the base who’s base the player wishes to steal from. Player must role 1d6 if even number player may succeed and must role an  additional 1d6 for which material stolen, and a 1d6 for quantity. If on first role  player roles an odd number player fails to steal. At the end of stealing that  token is frozen for duration of players round. 
o Gather Materials: Only the Triceratops has the ability to gather materials. In  order to collect materials a Player must vocalize his action by stating that she  wishes to gather materials. Player may then stay at specific location and  continually gather materials. Beginning on players next turn, player will  automatically role for that material space. There are two types of material  spaces. 
• Mine spaces: contains Iron, crystals, and oil 
• Mill space: contains lumber and Gohjah Berries 
▪ Rolling 1d6 for Mines: 1-3 = Iron, 4-5 = crystal, 6 = oil 
▪ Rolling 1d6 for Mills: odd = lumber, even = Gohjah Berries 
o Build: Players may choose to build from either their time machine blue print  card, or from a modification blue print card. This will take up TWO player  actions. For time machine, player will flip over corresponding card. Players  MUST turn in expended material tokens to the designated ‘banker’. 
o Play event card: Player will receive event cards during the course of the game.  Event cards come in many flavors. Players are to follow the direction on the  event card.
• During the course of the game, a player may choose to land a follower on an event  space. In this occurrence, the player must draw from the event card deck. There are  Two types of event cards: Action cards and Item cards. See below for descriptions of  each. 
o Action cards () are governed by what is on the card. Player must read allowed  the card and do what it says. After card is played, player must discard the card. o Items cards: () refers to any card that gives a player materials. After card is read,  player must discard the card. 
• A player will want to increase their Dino-cult following in order to collect materials,  attack, and defend against other players. This is done by one of two ways: choosing to  influence their cult, or by drawing a corresponding event card. 
o When a player chooses to Influence her cult they must role a 1d20. Results as  follows: a role of 1 to 3 (-1) follower, 4 to 10 nothing happens, 11 to 15 plus one  follower, 16 to 20 plus 2 followers. 
o Player must vocalize to influence either T-rex or triceratops before role. 
• Combat rules: The offense player vocalizes which token he will attack. Account for all  modifiers on both sides. Without modifications offense roles 1d6. Triceratops are not  cannot attack, they can only defend. A Triceratops roles a 1d8 without modifications. Highest lives lowest dies. For each tie, Players must role again.  
o Players may build either a laser beam (only for T-Rex) or an energy shield (only  for Triceratops). 
▪ Laser beam blue print: 2 oil, 1 crystal, 2 gohjah berries 
▪ Energy shield blue print: 2 oil, 3 crystal, 2 gohjah berries 
o Players may build as many of each as they would like. Player may put on any  Dinosaur. 
Actions- 
• Move 
• Attack 
• Trade 
• Steal 
• gather materials 
• Build 
• play event card 
• Influence 
• Role Dice (INV) 
• Draw card (INV) 
• Defend (INV) 
Transitions- 
• End player turn 
• Next player turn 
• Combat 
• Communication of trade 
• End game
• Modify Dinosaur 
Items- 
• Game Board 
• 2 D6 dice per player 
• 1 D20 dice per game 
• 1 D8 dice per game 
• 1D4 dice per game 
• Event Cards (30) 
• Time Machine blue print cards (20) 
• Material chips (17 blue, 25 red, 20 green, 12 black, 15 white) 
• Triceratops Dinosaur tokens (10 blue, 10 red, 10 green, 10 black) 
• T-rex Dinosaur tokens (10 blue, 10 red, 10 green, 10 black) 
• Reference Cards (4) 
Setup- 
• Make sure all Items are included with set (see Items list) 
• Establish number of players 
• On game board place 30 event cards in center of volcano 
• One player shuffle Time machine cards and begin dealing cards to players in a counter clockwise fashion one card each rotation until all players have exactly 5 time-machine  blue print cards. 
• Give each player 2 1d6 dice 
• Place 1 d20 beside event card deck 
• Assign players a character colors (this will determine which color tokens they receive) • Give each player 2 T-Rex tokens according to their assigned color, these must be place  on each player respective base 
• Give each player 2 Triceratops tokens according to their assigned color, these must be  placed on each player respective base 
• Give each player a reference card 
• Assign one player the role of materials keeper (refer to glossary) 
• Assign one player the role of Dinosaur Keeper (refer to glossary) 
• Begin game (refer to rules)

FLOWCHARTS


Meaningful Action Analysis (MAA) 
• Move- Player decides where to move in accordance to rule limitations (refer to rules).  This is a meaningful action as the player choose where to move within allotted  parameters. 
• Attack- Player may choose to attack another player’s dinosaur given that the dinosaur is  in the space next to the other. This is a meaningful action for the player. 
• Trade- Player may choose to trade with another player. This offers a dynamic for of a  tradeoff that involves meaningful decision for both parties. 
• Steal- The steal action is a meaningful decision that has the capacity to raise tensions  between players. 
• gather materials- The player has the option of what materials to gather and where to  gather specific materials. The player must strategize on proper placement as some  places may be more prone to attack by other players 
• Build- Building on either the time machine or modifications for dinosaur is a meaning  full decision. Timing important and is as well a key factor in strategy. Players must  maintain balance of modifications to dinosaurs in order to attack and defend. Player  must also keep in mind their main goal of completing their time machine. 
• play event card- A player may receive an actionable event card at some point, it is up to  the player on when the best time to play that card will be. 
• Influence- In order to grow a players Dino-cult following the player must use the  influence action. As growth is essential for game flow the player must decide on when  to use this action strategically. 
• Role Dice (INV)- This is a direct effect of combat or a specific event card, therefore it is  not meaningful. 
• Draw card (INV)- This is a direct effect based on a character’s choice of movement, but  in essence it is not a meaningful decision as the player does not choose which card she  gets. 
• Defend (INV)- This is an automatic occurrence initiated by combat. If another player  attacks the other must defend.
Board Design 
**Created with InKarnate- http://inkarnate.com/ 

Component Pieces 
• Game Board 
• 2 D6 dice per player 
• 1 D20 dice per game 
• 1 D8 dice per game 
• 1D4 dice per game 
• Event Cards (30) 
• Time Machine blue print cards (20) 
• Material chips (17 blue, 25 red, 20 green, 12 black, 15 white) 
• Triceratops Dinosaur tokens (10 blue, 10 red, 10 green, 10 black) 
• T-rex Dinosaur tokens (10 blue, 10 red, 10 green, 10 black) 
• Reference Cards (4)

DETAILED DESIGN & TESTING – ASSIGNMENT 3 
Play Testers Stage 1 
Play Test 1: In-person 
• total number testing: 1 
• Ben, age: 24, male 
• I performed my first play test with one of my roommates. He is a talented musician  as well as an aspiring music producer. He enjoys strategy games, and in the past has  put a few together himself. 
Test Stage 1 Results 
Play test one: 
• My established purpose for my first play through was aimed at testing how fast a player  was able to pick up the rules, average length of game play, and testing out the gathering  materials mechanic. I used my roommate Ben for this play test. 
I found that that the order in which I presented the game rules hindered game play by  30 minutes. When the game started, I had to elaborate on certain aspects, as well as  cover certain rules that I left out. One big issue that I ran across was due to my 
neglecting to provide information on both how the material chip system relates to  materials, and how roles dictate which material a player will receive. However, I did  develop a very good method that remedied this oversight during game play. Ben quickly  picked up on the game very rapidly after 25 minutes into game play. While he did enjoy  the game, we did both agree that the game was better suited for 3-4 players. There was  very little player to player interaction with one other person, which is pertinent for the  exploration of certain mechanics. This did not inhibit focus on the gathering materials  mechanic. I found this mechanic to work particularly well for player usability, but can be  vastly improved upon to increase the dynamics across the entire game. 
• Photos of play test on gameplay: 
** explaining the rules about to Start my first playtest
**Ben picked up the game pretty fast, and flowed pretty nicely after about 30 minute in 
**Ben is on his last blue print card (he ended up beating me in the end) 
Play test two: 
• The purpose of this play test is to examine the attack action more closely, to examine  prompt clarity in event cards, and to focus on ideas to increase game dynamics and  flow.
The game played out very smoothly in comparison to the last iteration. There was very little  elaboration of rules needed to start the game. We found that it is more effective to play a  two-player game side by side rather than across from one another. This allowed us more  opportunity for attacking engagements. We did not get to examine attack in our last  iteration. Game play progressed smoothly with little time taken per turn. I found myself  implementing actions more smoothly as well, becoming more comfortable with the game.  However, game play was not without flaw. Many of the prototyped cards can be better  written, or elaborated upon. I found that mountains are not clearly marked as off limits, of I  plan to implement a rule stating that all hexagonal spaces containing only mountain are off  limits. Clarification on blue prints is needed. We did not get to experiment with the laser or  shield blue print cards; which increase combat odds by adding an additional 6-sided die.  However, we did get to engage one another in combat quite a few times. I asked ben how  he felt about the mechanic, he seemed to like it for its simplicity.

** a little bit in to our 2nd iteration, I found that with two people it is better to be beside each other  rather than across
**I have deliberately influenced many of the t-rex to better test out combat

*we are able to interact more closely now, which allows for more dynamic strategy 
Play test 3: 
• The main purpose of this iteration was to test more on the stealing and trading  mechanics. I also ended up tweaking the amount of materials available to simulate a  lack of recourses. During this play through we were able to play through very smoothly  in comparison to the last two tests. During game play, we discussed many Ideas for  better mechanics. One example is the terrain features in the game offer no limitation to  movement, and we both agreed that it would be a very fun way to increase strategic  edge to game play. I told him it is something I will have to think on, it will require a lot of  “tweaking” within other mechanics as well to work efficiently. This play through only  took about an hour and ten minutes. I am debating on whether or not to create options 
to game play, similar to that of pandemic. In which players can choose how hard they  want the game to be and also how long they want the game to take. I was able to test  the stealing action twice. I asked ben how he felt about it. He felt that it was pretty  simple and simple is good for a mechanic such as stealing. I would like to experiment  with a 4-sided die, as we currently use a 6-sided die. The number dictates the amount of  materials stolen. This is role after each player roles a six-sided die, this part works very  similarly to rolling for attack. Over all I am quite pleased with game play, and I am  working on a player rule set going off what I have taken away from these three  playtests. 

** I am quite pleased with how well the blue print cards create unique strategy ( see ben top left  examining his blue print card)

** I finally was able to get into position to steal from bens base. I was able to steal two times.

**Ben wins yet again, victory nap 
Test Stage 1 Reflection 
At the end of our tests, I asked ben to give me three good things, three bad things, and a brief  summary of his overall take on the game. For the good he wrote: in general, the game is very tightly  constructed, the game is very fun to play, and it is easy to learn. For the bad he wrote: there are some  pretty repetitive mechanics, Illogical story with too many “plot holes”, requires minimum of 3 players for  engaging game play. For his brief summary, he wrote: I recommend expanding on the mechanics to  more fluidly integrate lasers and create new event cards. Balance the event cards to make the game fair. 
I will focus on the bad comments as well as suggestions within his summary. I feel that for  repetitive mechanics we mutually agree on. There are many ways to improve the mechanics that take 
form in simply change die roles and which type of die used. Example for stealing I can use the 4-sided  die instead of 6 so stealing materials does not come off as too harsh for other players. I can adjust  numbers on event cards (which I made sure to write in pencil). For Illogical story with too many “plot  holes”, I neglected to clarify how he actually meant that. But what I think he means is that I neglected  some of core elements of what the game is about. Which I totally agree on that. The game is about mad  scientist and dinosaurs, but players don’t really assume the role of the scientists they play as  themselves. In monopoly people “fight” over their favorite piece like me personally I like the car. That  subtle edge is just one example that make games remember able and thus replay able. I feel like my  game is a tad underdeveloped in that way. But I can easily fix this in ways such as giving these mad  scientists names or how I word the player rules. I do agree with him on the number of players as well,  which he was actually genuinely excited to play with more people. This is an easy fix, just have more  people, I will be able to elaborate more on this aspect in stage two. I am with him on the laser beams as  well, it has been bugging me about the lack of laser beams in a game titled Mad Scientists and Dinosaurs  with Laser Beams. I plan on making changes to this in later play tests. I will need to try this in a variety of  ways to see which works best. 
There were several things I spotted, and took note of. Many of the cards do not clarify certain  details that I had to elaborate on frequently. There are certain flaws within the blue print cards  mechanics. There are certain aspects of trade that I need to address. There is a certain level of fairness  that needs to be reach, so players don’t feel like they are at a disadvantage.  
Card clarity pertains to both balancing and layout. I can easily fix these issues by recreating the  cards, introducing additional cards, and re writing cards that offer complete clarification. The blue print  cards in themselves work very well, but there are certain rules that I need to address. I have not  documented that the blue print cards are be laid faced down the exception being the left most card,  upon completion of one card you are able to look at the next, going left to right. I initially promoted  trading as a simple action, but noticed that additional rules needed to be implemented. Such as one  trade action counts as one proposition to an individual player, and cannot be extended to the group as a  whole. Balancing fairness I examined in certain aspects of the game, such as: distance of material spaces  from individual bases. I can play around with different forms of player movement like, terrain features.  This will require experimentation, as I am not going to recreate the game board. I will have to find my  way around this through trial and error. 
As mentioned in my first playtest It took me a while to explain the game to get things rolling.  Eventually it all clicked with ben and we were able to play through relatively smoothly. I did have to  make up certain rules that were over looked on the fly, such as what chip equals which material. This  also extends to the stealing action, and setting mountain boundaries. These questions and oversights  did indeed diminish. The last play through went by without much flaw. The only questions asked mainly  pertaining to which die role give you which material. 
Detailed Rules for Players 
Game Summary: 
Mad Scientist and Dinosaurs with Laser-beams is a Jurassic themed strategy board  game. Players assume the role of one of four mad scientists stuck in the Jurassic period armed  only with their universal linguistic trans modulator (ULTM) allowing communication with  dinosaurs and their knowledge on how to build a time machine (as well as laser-beams). Players  control various Dinosaurs from within their scientists “Dino-Cult” in-order to collect materials  from the Jurassic world or from other scientists. Players must use the materials to be the first  scientist to complete their time machine. 
Contents 
o Gameboard 
▪ On each side of the game board there is a player base (represented by  six brown hexagonal spaces set in a circle around either an orange or  grey hexagonal space), there are four bases. There are three types of  
special spaces scattered throughout the rest of the board. The three  
types of spaces are as follows: Mining spaces represented by a shovel,  milling spaces represented by an axe, and event cards represent by a  yellow rectangle. These spaces will be discussed in the player turns  
section. There are various terrain features. The mountains are off  
limits. Trees, hills and planes will be discussed during movement. 
o Dinosaur Pieces 
▪ Each player is able to control two types of dinosaurs, a triceratops and  a T-rex. The Triceratops is represented by the orange pieces. The T 
rex is represented by the green pieces. Each piece is marked by a  
colored pin (yellow, blue, green, or red). Each colored pin represents a  specific player. Dinosaur pieces will be discussed in more detail in the  Player turns section. 
o Material Chips- Materials are represented by one of five colored chips (green,  red, blue, white, or black).  
Setup 
o On game board place 30 event cards in center of volcano 
o One player shuffle Time machine cards and begin dealing cards to players in a counter clockwise fashion one card each rotation until all players have exactly 5 time-machine  blue print cards. 
o Give each player 2 1d6 dice 
o Place 1 d20 beside event card deck 
o Assign players a character colors (this will determine which color tokens they receive) o Give each player 2 T-Rex tokens according to their assigned color, these must be place  on each player respective base 
o Give each player 2 Triceratops tokens according to their assigned color, these must be  placed on each player respective base 
o Give each player a reference card 
o Assign one player the role of materials keeper (refer to glossary) 
o Assign one player the role of Dinosaur Keeper (refer to glossary) 
Player Turns 
o Actions: Each player turn consists of five action points. There are six  available actions a player may choose from: movement, Influence, trade,  attack, steal, and build (described below). Each of these actions- with the  exception of build- take up one action point. Build takes up two action points.
Each player may perform any combination of these actions (for example see  movement). During the course of the game players will gather materials. This  is done at the beginning of a players turn automatically, and requires no  action points. Gather materials will be discussed further at the bottom of this  section. 
▪ Movement: For a Triceratops each hexagonal space a dinosaur piece is moved is  equivalent to one action point. For a T-Rex two hexagonal spaces a dinosaur  piece is moved is equivalent to one action point (note: if player choose to move  T-rex only one space it still counts as one action point). The only exception to  this is each individual players’ base, which a player may move pieces around in  freely. A player’s only limitations to movement are: another players base, the  perimeter of the board map, a space occupied by another dinosaur. and  hexagonal spaces completely covered by a mountain. Movement may be  performed in combination with other actions. Example: player moves two  spaces, attacks, and with her fourth action point trades with another player.  
▪ Influence: A player will want to increase their “Dino-cult following” (dinosaur  pieces) in order to collect more materials, attack, and defend against other  players. This is done by the influence action. For this action, the player must  verbalize which dinosaur type to influence (Triceratops or T-rex). The player will  then role the twenty-sided die. The outcome corresponds to the following roles:  1-3 one dinosaur leaves, 4-9 no dinosaur is gained no dinosaur leaves, 10- 16  player gains one dinosaur, 17-20 player gains 2 dinosaurs. Note, that player  must gain or lose the type of dinosaur they vocalized beforehand. 
▪ Trade: players may trade materials between other players on an individual  basis. Example: if a player makes an offer to another player and that player  declines, that offer may not be extended to an additional player. This would  count as two action points. 
▪ Attack: a player may choose to attack another player’s follower. Followers must  be within attacking range. For modified attack see mod blue prints for  specifications.  
• The offense player vocalizes which token he will attack. Account for all  modifiers on both sides. Without modifications offense roles 1d6.  
Triceratops cannot attack, they can only defend. A Triceratops roles a  1d8 without modifications. Highest lives lowest dies. For each tie,  
Players must role again.  
• Players may build either a laser beam (only for T-Rex) or an energy  shield (only for Triceratops). 
• Laser beam blue print: 2 oil, 1 crystal, 2 gohjah berries 
• Energy shield blue print: 2 oil, 3 crystal, 2 gohjah berries 
• Players may build as many of each as they would like. Player may put on  any Dinosaur. 
▪ Steal: Steal: In order for a player to steal the player must have a follower in an  adjacent space to the base who’s base the player wishes to steal from.  Player must role 1d6 if even number player may succeed and must role an  additional 1d6 for which material stolen, and a 1d6 for quantity. If on first role  player roles an odd number player fails to steal. At the end of stealing that  token is frozen for duration of players round.
▪ Build: Build: Players may choose to build from either their time machine blue  print card, or from a modification blue print card. This will take up TWO player  actions. For time machine, player will flip over corresponding card. For all other  blue prints, Player will place back into the event deck (reshuffle cards). Players  MUST turn in expended material tokens to the designated ‘banker’. 
o Gathering Materials: Only the Triceratops has the ability to gather materials. In  order to collect materials players must move the Dinosaurs token into the  
corresponding material space. to collect materials a Player must vocalize his  
action by stating that she wishes to gather materials. Player may then stay at  
specific location and continually gather materials. Beginning on players next  
turn, player will automatically role for that material space at the beginning of  
her turn. There are two types of material spaces. 
• Mine spaces (represented by a shovel): contains Iron, crystals,  
and oil 
• Mill space (represented by an axe): contains lumber and Gohjah  
Berries 
▪ Rolling 1d6 for Mines: 1-3 = Iron, 4-5 = crystal, 6 = oil 
▪ Rolling 1d6 for Mills: odd = lumber, even = Gohjah Berries 
Play Testers Stage 2 
Play test stage 2: game one 
• Katie, age 21, female, in-person. 
o Katie is also one of my roommates, she like to do art, and read. She is not very  board game savvy. An excellent person for testing how my player rules are  
interpreted. 
• Adam, age 23, male, in-person 
o I have in the past play D&D with Adam. He is able to pick up on complex rules  easily. He is best person I know to gain feedback on game mechanics. 
• Ben, age 24, male, in-person 
o He is a talented musician as well as an aspiring music producer. He enjoys  
strategy games, and in the past, has put a few together himself.  
Play test stage 2: game two 
• Laney, age 19, female, in-person 
o Laney is not big on playing board game, she is more of a reading a book kind of  gal. She will also be a good person to test how my rules make sense. 
• Brantley, age 20, male, in-person 
o He has not picked up a board game in years, he prefers the good ol’ Alabama  outdoors.  
• Chris, age 19, male, in-person 
o He plays poker a good bit, not big on board games. But he seems to be a quick  learner. 
• Nick, age 21, male, in person
o Don’t know him very well however, from talking to him he seems quite  
intelligent. Does not play board games on the regulars but is a quick learner. 
Test Stage 2 Results 
Play test one: 
This went pretty smoothly, granted I did have ben for this iteration as well. Regardless, I  found that my rules may need adjusting for further clarification, as well there are portions that need to  be edited out. As mentioned in early playtest Ben and I agreed that playing with more than two players  would be a blast! We were correct in our assumption. The main reason we thought this was because of  limited player interaction. I found that there is other way to increase this as well. For all materials, I  provided 25 chips of each. The abundance of recourses seemed to hinder player interaction in a fashion.  I started to experiment with limiting recourses. Taking away certain amounts of each. This intern caused  players to steal, attack, and trade more often. I was pleased with the dynamic strategies among players.  Laser beams and shields seemed to not appeal to the group as much as I would like. Being that they cost  materials which hinder building time machines. The rules all together seemed to click after a bit, but I  still need to work on those. I did receive clarification statements on movement from time to time. None  of which raised major issues. I took note on these however in order to add further clarification to the  player rules and documentation. Over all the game went well. 
** After starting they quickly dominated the board with pieces, lots of player interaction
**Katie is contemplating her next move 
**It was a pretty close call, but Katie ended up winning. 
Play test two: 
I had my friend Laney recruit 3 of her friends, whom I had never met. They were all pretty  enthusiastic about playing, which I was pleased to take note of. I really wanted to experiment further  with the limitations of recourses on this iteration. I decided to decrease oil to 12 chips, crystal to 15, iron  to 17, gojah berries I kept at 25, lumber to 20. I experienced good feedback on this, players felt it 
simulated scarcity very well. It really influenced dynamic decisions throughout the game. There was  minimal confusion across game play. Clarification was only prominent in the beginning of the game. I  reminded them to be looking for areas they felt need to be improved upon. At first, they seemed  reluctant to give anything but good feedback. When I told them that good feedback is good, but bad  feedback is great because that tells me where I need to improve, they really stepped up and provided  some helpful advice. Throughout the game, I noticed some pretty interesting strategy. One example was  when Nick started trading with people saying he needed lumber. Everyone assumed that is what he  needed to complete his last blue print to win the game, so no one would make the trade. After several  turns of this he made the same offer, which was declined. He then changed his offer, asking to trade for  crystals. Turns out he was bluffing the entire time and he won right then and there. I enjoyed seeing the  twist and turns a game I created could make among people outside of my influence. 
** Progress was a little slow at the start but, all four of them got competitive fast

** It seems that nick has the advantage on the game (referring to the amount of the blue print cards he  has left)

**Nick performed a bluff during trading which fooled everyone, He ended up winning  
Test Stage 2 Reflection 
Laney had mentioned that she would really like to see actual dinosaur pieces rather than my  prototypes pieces. Chris felt that he was at a disadvantage starting out on his side, he had further to  travel to reach recourse spaces. All the players would like to see more event cards. Nick felt that it  would help all players if they had a reference cards for specific parts of the game. 
While I would like to incorporate actual dinosaur pieces, I do not feel my game is at that stage  yet. If I continued to develop this game beyond this class, I would buy a 3-d printer to accomplish this. 
Which I’m planning to get any way. So, in all actuality her advice was pretty good. I am still thinking on a  way to implement a more dynamic movement mechanic, hope fully in a way that will decrease  disadvantages like the one Chris outlined. Hopefully the next iteration will explore those mechanics. I  am still working on creating and cataloging more event cards, I find this to be a huge issue and will be  fixed in future iterations. Nicks Idea of reference cards was pretty on point. It had crossed my mind in  the past. But this will defiantly be introduced in my next iteration. 
I’m still having trouble getting people to utilize the lasers and energy shields. The rules may  need to be implemented around the event card space. There are some modifications I can make to the  triceratops to be more appealing. The game lasted close to three hours. 
Lasers and energy shields seemed to still be disregarded, I am thinking about taking them out of the  event card draw and implementing them into standard play issue but increasing the cost of materials  require to produce them. With the event card space a player can easily just bounce back and forth on  the space. I am not sure if this is a fair component or not. I will most likely have created more negative  cards in the event cards or possible establish a rule against it. The triceratops only ability is to search for  materials and defend against attack. I feel that It may be necessary to make drastic changes in ability or  movement. The play through lasted three hours which was the longest round yet, and the only play  through with 4 people. I will need to see in later iteration if I can modify parts of the game to contribute  to quicker game play. 
They worked for the first play through better, the main reason because ben was able to clarify  amongst the rest of player. For this last one they needed some guidance and clarification. I need to  make a printed copy so it is easy for them to reference themselves. 
Trading had to be clarified, because the rules I had left some scenarios in question. Stealing was  not a big issue, but I caused some confusion because I wanted them to experiment with the four-sided  die. There were numerous parts that I also need to clarify, which I took note of to implement in future  iterations. 
DETAILED DESIGN & TESTING – ASSIGNMENT 4 
New Mechanic 
I will be implementing a new mechanic to my game. This mechanic will take advantage of terrain  features that already pre-exist on the board map. I will refer to these key terrain features this additional  mechanic will take advantage of as: planes, forests, and hills. The mountains that exist have already  been incorporated into the game rules as “off limits”. The planes will be defined as any hexagonal space  that are completely covered in grass texture. The forests will be defined as any hexagonal space which  contain partial to full tree objects. The hill will be defined as any hexagonal space which contains partial  to full hill objects. This new mechanic will directly affect two other existing mechanics: the movement  mechanic as well as the action points mechanic. During game play if a player move through either a hill  or a forest they will do so at the cost of two action points. The player’s movement on planes will cost 1  action point. The goal of this new mechanic is to increase the games dynamics by creating a tradeoff type decision. This new mechanic will help to increase the amount of thought a player must put into his  or her strategy. Example strategy: isolating a triceratops behind forest to safely mine materials by using  trees as a buffer. 
Play Testers Stage 3 
Play Test Stage 3: In-person 
• total number testing: 1 
• Ben, age: 24, male 
• I performed my first play test with one of my roommates. He is a talented musician  as well as an aspiring music producer. He enjoys strategy games, and in the past, has  put a few together himself.  
Test Stage 3 Results 
Play test 1: 
For my first playtest in stage three my testing goal was to test out my additional  mechanic that I added to the game. My additional mechanic, the terrain feature (refer to above  ‘New Mechanic’) as Ben described “seemed like an issue at first, but worked fluidly”. He also  made a point to suggest that it might be a good idea to allow additional action points with the  new mechanic as a factor. We both agreed that this new rule can potentially hinder game play  otherwise. Problems did arise from this new mechanic regarding clarity in movement. Example:  when I moved onto a material space that was outside of the woods- but with trees still partially  on the hexagonal space, we had to discuss if that would count as two action points. I did  address this issue in the New Mechanic description prior, but there are special cases where this  might still be confusing. He also expressed his thoughts on the Dinosaurs combat interaction, of  which he stated: “could use some experimentation and fine tuning”.
Pic 1.1 
At the beginning of the game I  described the new terrain  mechanic Ben
Pic 1.2 
There was clarification needed  for the new mechanic  
whenever a dinosaur left a  forest or hill.
Pic 1.3 
The game started to pick up  
after clearing up some of the  
confusion.
Play test 2: 
For my second playtest, I further tested the terrain mechanic but with the addition of an  extra action point. I also tested some changes that I made to the combat system. The T-rex uses  1d6 and an additional 1d6 with the laser modifier just as before. However, the triceratops uses  a 1d8 and an additional 1d4 with the shield modifier. The play through went pretty smoothly  with the addition of 1 action point. As for the combat system; while I was comfortable with how  the change in dice balanced the combat system, I still need come up with some additional Ideas  to implement that complement the change. I feel that it might be necessary to tune some of  the other mechanics that effect the combat mechanic. One idea that I took note of was  extending the movement of the T-Rex to two movement spaces instead of one. In general, the  game went well. Ben expressed his approval with both the change of dice for combat, and the  extra action point for player turns. 
Pic 2.1 
With the addition of an extra  action point we found movement  nowhere near as restricting.
2.2 
I found that it is rather  hard to flank with so  much terrain between  us.
2.3 
I am quite impressed  
with the influence  
mechanic. The  
numbers I set seem to  
balance well.
For my third play test, I will be focusing on the two changes that I made. The main goal  of these changes is to increase combat appeal in the game, and also to increase movement  around the board. I extended the movement of T-Rex’s to two spaces per action point. I also  lowered the amount of materials required to build both the laser and shield. As well, I took all  laser and shield blue prints out of the action card draw. Instead players will have full access to  building lasers and shields in game. I should also mention that for this iteration, we played with  three time machine blue print cards instead of five. I plan on offering this as a choice during set  up to control the amount of time each game takes. The play through went well with the  additional tuning. Lowering the cost of building modifications increased the appeal of  expending the resources. The increased movement towards the T-Rex increased the games  intensity, as the T-rex could rapidly attack. With the tuning I had done to the Triceratops in the  last iteration die roles, I found balanced the game really well.  
3.1 
I am coming around on  the left to flank Ben
3.2  
I was able to kill two  
Triceratops before he killed  me with a T-Rex. He then  landed on an event card. A  big meteor killed his T-Rex.

Test Stage 3 Reflection 
3.3 
I finally got the chance to go  crazy with my laser beam. Then  He killed me with his laser  beam.

The additional terrain mechanic was hindered by board layout. The dinosaur characters  were unbalance and under developed. Resources appear extra scarce when surrounded by  terrain. Players frequently had to refer to rules for confirmation of materials and dice roles. 
Due to the layout of the board, issues in clarity arose in regards to cost of action points.  While I had thought about the issue before hand, certain situations still promoted those issues.  I adjusted the wording in the rules accordingly. Players found that the balance of dinosaurs was  a bit bland; as well, players felt that the triceratops was at a disadvantage. Throughout the third  
stage I tuned some mechanics to help with this issue. I started by changing the dice rolled by  the Triceratops during combat, giving them 1d8 with an additional 1d4 with the shield modifier.  I decreased the amount of materials required to build lasers and shield, making them more  accessible. I provided the t-rex with an additional space per movement to balance the 1d8 given  to the Triceratops. Players found that terrain features hindered gathering material in certain  areas. I found it hard to adjust the terrain mechanic to this issue due to the permanent nature  
of the board. I will continue to think of ways to fix this issue for further iteration. Even though  the game rules are understood by players, there is some information that is hard to remember.  I will be adding a “cheat sheet” card for further iterations. This will include information on roles,  materials, and the blue prints for shield/lasers mods. 
I found the game to last too long in certain game sessions. Blue print cards that I pulled  from the event card deck need to be replaced with additional event cards. Stealing is rarely  used. There are better ways to go about the Dino-cult. 
I found that the game can potentially last far too long. I feel that it Is necessary to  implement a rule that allows players to decide on how long they want to take. I made a simple  change to the set-up which allows players to choose if they want to use 3 blue print cards or 5.  Although I played this stage with one other person, during a 3 to 4 player game event cards  would run out rapidly. I need to create additional event cards to replace the blue print cards I  took out. The event cards offer many elements to game play, having variety of event cards is  necessary for further iterations. During stage 3 neither of us ever attempted to steal from one  another. This is not a major issue, especially in a two-player game. I feel that their might be a  better way to tune this mechanic, as it has the potential to create great interaction. I will  continue to think on how to approach this in the future. I think it might greatly appeal to  players if there was a wider variety of dinosaurs to choose from. Having only two types of  dinosaurs is kind of lame in a Jurassic game. I will not get around to making those changes  during this class. However, if in the future, I will be more thoughtful in regards offering players  variety. 
I like the additional mechanic I tested during these tests. The only real big issue I had  with it is that I had not planned for such a mechanic while constructing my game board. I am  glad that I was able to make use of the terrain on the board- if I ever made the board again I  would certainly factor in this issue. I will continue to keep this new mechanic, since the issue is  
minor and can be fixed by a simple game board edit. 
The game is quite complex, I am surprised it works well with so many elements. While  playing with Ben, the only time we needed to look at the rules was to confirm materials  corresponding to dice roles, and the new mechanic. I think what makes it work well is while the  game is complex due to the number of mechanics; the actual mechanics are simple and easy to  understand. 
Play Testers Stage 4 
• Katie, age 21, female, in-person. 
o Katie is also one of my roommates, she like to do art, and read. She is not very  board game savvy. An excellent person for testing how my player rules are  
interpreted. 
• Adam, age 23, male, in-person 
o I have in the past play D&D with Adam. He is able to pick up on complex rules  easily. He is best person I know to gain feedback on game mechanics. 
• Ben, age 24, male, in-person 
o He is a talented musician as well as an aspiring music producer. He enjoys  
strategy games, and in the past, has put a few together himself. 
Test Stage 4 Results 
Play test 1 
For this play test, I wanted to focus on the terrain mechanic outside of my influence and  with a group of 3 people. I also wanted to see how the new rules in combat effected player  interaction. I was pleased with how smoothly things went. Granted, this group had played in  earlier stages. I received a lot of good feedback from Katie and Adam, they both agreed that it  was a good improvement compared to the last time they played. There were a few issues  resolved by Ben- mainly dealing with the same problems from the last stage (game board map).  All the changes I made to increase combat interaction- and interaction in general- paid off. The  exception being a lack of laser and shield production. I intended to create reference cards for  this round, though I neglected to do so. I will be including those in my next session. 

1.1 
Katie and Adam start their first play through 
with my additional rules and mechanics.

1.2 
Katie used her 5 action points to influence. With the help of some lucky die roles she  effectively beefs up her Dino-Cult.

1.3 
Adam is a bit salty about his last few turns. He lost many trying to influence. He is finally  bouncing back.
Play test 2: 
For my final play test, I focused on how reference cards improved fluid game play. All  players enjoyed having a card with all information including: roles, materials, and the blue  prints for shield/lasers mods. I found that game play picked up pace with the additional asset,  as players did not have to ask questions for reassurance. This time around players did branch  out with lasers and shields, I was able to confirm that the dice roles work well. No one felt that  they were at a disadvantage. Movement across the board increased during this game, as  players invested more time with the T-rex and combat. 

2.1 
The red cards are the reference cards I introduced to this iteration.

2.2 
For this game, we used three cards instead of 5. So far Ben has  
won every game, it looks like Katie may have the upper hand.  
though 

2.3 
Ben attempted a trade with Katie. She declined. During her next turn she cashed  in for her time machine. Winning the game.
Test Stage 4 Reflection 
The players expressed positive feedback on the additional mechanics. Players did agree  that the board could use re- designing.  
All players liked the additional mechanics which include: the terrain mechanic, alternate  dice roles, and increased movement for T-rex pieces. They did express that the board could use  an update in design to accommodate for the terrain features. I agree with them, and if I were to  continue this project in the future I would add such changes. 
It bothers me that I did not implement scientists into the game as well as I would have  liked. Outside of the rules, there is no element of the game that engages the players as  “scientists”. That set aside, players really did have fun playing my game. 
The title of the game is Mad Scientists and Dinosaurs with Laser-Beams. I feel like I did  not adequately portray scientists in game. In future iterations, I would like to include a unique  marker for every scientist. Each marker would be in the shape of that scientist’s crazy mad  scientist hair; as well, each marker would be one of four colors. On the back of the marker  describes that scientist. Although trivial, it would add an additional element to the game  (similar to the attachment one get over a specific monopoly piece example: I will fight you for  the cannon). 
The terrain mechanic was well liked. I plan on keeping it, but the only way for me to  adequately balance the game with it is to re design the map of the board game. I feel that my game does a good job at utilizing simple mechanics, which makes it easy  to grasp. Granted there is a bit of a learning curve, but that’s due to the amount of mechanics  in it. It does seem complex at first because of that amount. I fixed most of the problems I did  have with the reference card, players found that to be a big help. Most of the remaining  problems in clarity have to do with the map, of which I have already made known. 
Final Reflection 
I am somewhere along the lines of excited and frightened when it comes to creating future design  documentation. These documents are right at the core of game design. This being my first one, I can  already see how complex they will become. I am glad that my first one was for an analog game. I learned  a great deal from writing this document, but I feel that my greatest take away comes from what I  learned that I need to learn. I need to develop my technical writing skills, and more specifically I need to  learn how to write for programming. 
When I came up the idea for my game I was thinking about an episode from the show Rick and  Morty, then I later recalled Sid Meier’s mentioning his ‘Dinos Game’ that he tried to make, sometime  after that I added laser beams to the equation (I think I was think of Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon). That’s  how my Idea started. What made it grow; was the iterative design process. I learned that ideas can  come from anywhere but in order to produce those ideas into something tangible, I must plan, test, be willing to make changes, and be willing to take risks. 
There is definitely an art to playtest- on several levels. Besides the actual planning of the game,  playtesting also calls for coordination amongst player. It’s not easy to organize play testing events. I  found that I need to be quick on my feet, during the first stages of play test, I found players looking to  me for the rules. If I never made that rule I had to do so on the spot. I actually came up with some good  rules that way. Critics are your best friend while playtesting. If they tell you something is wrong or sucks,  they are doing you a favor. 
This class reminded me about version control, and game documentation can be shared and edited  through version control. The Flow charts I have seen used in examples of text book I have read. I know I  will be able to use them for anything from interactive narrative design to planning out coding. Outs side 
of class I have read many book on safari about game design. Most recently ‘Learning C# by Developing  Game in Unity’. He talks on the subject of flow charts and how to make programming easier by mapping  the game mechanics out much like we did for this project. In my last class we put together a video in  unity as well, but prior to that we worked with interactive narrative design.

The user is asking me to help turn this game design document into an image. This is a substantial document about a board game called "Mad Scientists and Dinosaurs with Laser-Beams" - it includes game rules, flowcharts, playtesting notes, and various design elements.

I need to clarify what they mean by "make this into an image" - do they want:
1. A visual summary/infographic of the game?
2. The game board design as an image?
3. A rules reference card?
4. Something else entirely?

Let me ask for clarification on what specifically they'd like visualized, since this is a 48-page document with many different components.Hey Ahab - this is a hefty game design doc. Before I dive in, what specifically are you looking to visualize?

A few directions this could go:

1. **Rules reference card** - the "cheat sheet" mentioned in the doc (dice rolls, materials, laser/shield blueprints)
2. **Game board redesign** - a new hex map that better accommodates the terrain mechanics
3. **Visual summary/infographic** - condensed overview of the game for pitching or quick reference
4. **Component mockups** - event cards, time machine blueprints, the actual card layouts
5. **Flowchart cleanup** - proper visual flowcharts for game flow, combat, etc.

What's the use case here? Is this for a portfolio piece, actually producing the game, or something else?

i want an epic picture of tiny dinosaurs fighting mad scientists on a geomangi style pop up like board this is meant for me to create using another ai service to render the photo so all i need from you is the instructions

Ahab wants me to create a detailed image prompt for an AI image generation service (like Midjourney, DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, etc.). He wants an epic scene of tiny dinosaurs fighting mad scientists on a geomagni-style pop-up board game aesthetic.

Let me craft a detailed, evocative prompt that captures:
- Tiny dinosaurs (T-Rex, Triceratops from the game)
- Mad scientists (wild hair, lab coats, that classic aesthetic)
- Geomagni/pop-up book style - this means 3D paper craft, layered, colorful, with that tactile quality
- Board game setting
- Epic battle feeling
- Laser beams (it's in the title!)

I should give him a well-structured prompt with style descriptors, lighting, composition notes - the kind of thing that works well with AI image generators.Got it. Here's a prompt you can feed into Midjourney, DALL-E, or whatever you're using:

---

**Primary Prompt:**

Epic miniature diorama battle scene, tiny T-Rex and Triceratops dinosaurs with glowing laser beams mounted on their backs fighting wild-haired mad scientists in lab coats, set on a colorful hexagonal game board that pops up like a 3D paper craft geomagni toy, volcanic terrain with forests and mountains as layered paper cutouts, dramatic lighting with laser beam streaks of neon red and blue, tilt-shift photography style giving miniature toy effect, vibrant saturated colors, whimsical yet intense action poses, scattered game pieces and dice, the board emerging from a flat surface like a pop-up book, studio lighting with soft shadows

---

**Style modifiers to append (pick based on your service):**

- `--style raw` (Midjourney)
- `isometric view, toy photography, macro lens, shallow depth of field`
- `paper craft aesthetic, layered cardstock, tactile textures`
- `8k, hyperdetailed, product photography`

---

**Negative prompt (if supported):**

`realistic dinosaurs, photorealistic humans, dark mood, horror, blurry, low quality`

---

Want me to tweak the tone (more comedic vs dramatic), adjust the composition, or break this into multiple scene variations?

Game Identity
Player must defeat waves of enemies while avoiding being destroyed in order to defeat
the level.
4
Design Pillars
● Defeat waves of approaching enemies.
● Navigate obstacles in order to traverse the level.
● Gain XP to unlock Skills and upgrades.

Summary
Set in a not so distant future, Steve J. Savage will kick Ass in an Earth controlled by a
powerful Alien race, and overrun by feral monsters.
Primary Mechanics
5
Key Features
Weapon
Upgrades
The player will be able to upgrade their base weapons (Light, heavy,
Power weapons)
Special Abilities
player can use L.Shift and MMB to activate a variety of special
abilities
Building system
Allows the player to create both walls and turrets, and traps
alsoallows the player to unlock additional
variations of all three
Arena Combat
Incloses player into an open arena stye environment where they
must defeat a set number of enemies
"ADD's" to move to next area of the level
Hazardous
Objects
Objects placed around the level that can harm the player as well as
used by the player strategically
Skill system allows players to improve their player attributes
Game Economy
Junk
Scraps:
● Collected throughout environment, chests, and recovered off of dead
enemies.
6
● Can be used to build up broken walls/ barriers
● Used to for some weapon upgrades.
Metal
● Collected throughout environment, chests, and recovered off of dead
enemies.
● Can be used to build and fix turrets.
● Used to for weapon upgrades.
Alien glands
● Alien glands provide the player with the ability to improve their attributes and/ or
abilities.
● Alien glands can be collected primarily by killing Alien and monsters enemies, and
can occasionally be recovered from chests and turrets.
Streaks
Streaks can be gained by eliminating a number of enemies without receiving damage. Once
the player starts a streak, they get a damage boost and slowly regenerate health. Once the
player takes damage the streak is broken.
